key_insight: The paper argues that ML/AI strategies for DFT must be matched to the specific bottleneck—XC functional, kinetic energy functional, density, or basis functions—because Kohn-Sham and orbital-free DFT, as well as electronic and nuclear DFT, have different accuracy and algorithmic needs.
engineering_relevance: This helps researchers choose ML models that improve DFT speed and usability without overfitting the wrong part of the physics, especially when targeting scalable electronic-structure and nuclear-structure simulations.
method: Use deep neural networks primarily for Kohn-Sham DFT tasks where they are most effective.
Prefer symbolic regression as a promising route for kinetic energy functionals and potentially for both electronic and nuclear DFT.
Transfer successful ML-enhanced electronic DFT ideas to nuclear DFT where applicable, while accounting for different constraints.
evidence_signal: The paper is a review/overview synthesizing prior research and comparing computational bottlenecks and accuracy requirements rather than presenting a new dataset or benchmark result.
watch_item: A key concern is that methods successful for exchange-correlation learning in KS DFT may perform poorly for kinetic energy functionals, so direct transfer of architectures without physics-aware adaptation can be misleading.
paper_title: ML and AI for density functional theory: different priorities for Kohn-Sham and orbital-free DFT, for electronic and nuclear DFT
